ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_03311737 covers milk tea as a general category. No specific low-fat variant code exists; closest_parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FKP covers tea-based beverages broadly. No specific low-fat milk tea code exists in FoodEx2; closest_parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226465004 represents milk tea as a substance category. Low-fat variant not separately coded; closest_parent used.

preparation_method: served chilled or at room temperature

serving.natural_language_portion: One large cup of low fat milk tea as typically served at KOI Thé and similar Taiwanese tea chains in Singapore, approximately 500 ml.

nutrient_source.title: Milk tea, prepared with low-fat milk — estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy: Tea, brewed (NDB 14355) and Milk, lowfat, fluid, 1% milkfat (NDB 01082), combined in approximate 60:40 ratio with added sugar at ~6 g per 100 ml
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct USDA or HPB entry for low-fat milk tea as a prepared beverage. Values estimated by combining USDA SR Legacy data for brewed black tea (NDB 14355) and 1% low-fat milk (NDB 01082) in a 60:40 volumetric ratio, plus approximately 6 g added sugar per 100 ml, consistent with standard Taiwanese tea-shop formulations. Energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, and secondary nutrients derived from this weighted combination. This is a medium-confidence estimate.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of milk tea estimated from analogous foods: black tea (GI ~0, negligible carbohydrate contribution) and full-fat milk (GI ~27, Atkinson et al. 2008) adjusted for added sugar (sucrose GI ~65); composite GI estimated at approximately 45 for sweetened low-fat milk tea
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for low-fat milk tea as served at Taiwanese tea shops. GI estimated at 45 (low category) using University of Sydney GI Database values for milk (GI 27) and sucrose (GI 65) weighted by carbohydrate contribution in the beverage. Glycemic load per 500 g serving = (45 × 49 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 22.1. This is a medium-confidence analogous estimate consistent with peer-reviewed GI methodology.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium content (~140 mg per 500 ml serving)
hypertension.guidance: Low fat milk tea contains approximately 140 mg sodium per large serving, which is within acceptable limits for hypertensive individuals, though added sugar content should be monitored as excess sugar intake can contribute to weight gain and secondary blood pressure elevation.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High added sugar content (~46 g per 500 ml serving) causing significant glycemic load
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should exercise caution with sweetened low fat milk tea, as a large serving delivers approximately 46 g of sugar and a glycemic load of ~22, which can cause significant postprandial blood glucose elevation; unsweetened or reduced-sugar options should be requested.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High added sugar content contributing to excess caloric intake and potential dyslipidaemia
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: While low fat milk tea has a reduced saturated fat content compared to full-fat variants (~1.5 g per 500 ml serving), the high added sugar content (~46 g per large serving) poses a moderate cardiovascular risk through potential contribution to hypertriglyceridaemia and excess caloric intake; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption or choose unsweetened versions.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Low Fat Milk Tea (Large) — known in Chinese as 低脂奶茶（大杯）— is a popular Taiwanese-style tea beverage served at specialty tea chains such as KOI Thé in Singapore. It is prepared by brewing black tea and blending it with low-fat (1%) milk and a sweetener, typically sugar syrup, resulting in a smooth, mildly creamy, lightly sweet beverage with a characteristic tea aroma. The large (L) size typically contains approximately 500 ml. Compared to full-fat milk tea variants, this version has a reduced fat content of approximately 0.5 g per 100 ml, making it a lighter dairy-based option. However, the beverage remains high in added sugars, contributing approximately 46–50 g of sugar per large serving, which gives it a moderate-to-high glycemic load (~22) despite a relatively low glycemic index (~45). Nutritionally, each 100 ml provides approximately 52 kcal, 1.4 g protein, 0.5 g fat, and 9.8 g carbohydrate, primarily from added sugar. The beverage provides modest amounts of calcium (~52 mg per 100 ml) and potassium (~95 mg per 100 ml) from the milk component. Clinically, this drink is of concern for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes due to its high sugar load, and should be consumed in moderation by those with cardiovascular disease. It is suitable for vegetarians and is generally considered halal. It is widely available at Taiwanese tea chains across Singapore and is a common afternoon or social beverage.

confidence_reason: No direct nutritional entry for low-fat milk tea as a prepared beverage exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy component data for brewed black tea and 1% low-fat milk combined in a 60:40 ratio with added sugar at approximately 6 g per 100 ml, consistent with standard Taiwanese tea-shop formulations. GI is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database values for milk and sucrose. Confidence is medium.
notes: Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy component data (brewed black tea NDB 14355 and 1% low-fat milk NDB 01082) in a 60:40 ratio with approximately 6 g added sugar per 100 ml. No direct HPB or USDA entry for this specific product exists. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero as low-fat milk and brewed tea contain negligible trans fats by formulation; confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy source data. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 — naturally zero as this is a liquid beverage with no fibrous components. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 — naturally zero as black tea and low-fat milk do not contribute meaningful vitamin C. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source data for this estimated combination. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source data for this estimated combination. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source data for this estimated combination. GI set to 45 (low category) as an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database values for milk (GI 27) and sucrose (GI 65). Glycemic load per 500 g serving = (45 × 49 g carbohydrate) / 100 = 22.1. This record represents the KOI Thé large low-fat milk tea as sold in Singapore; actual nutrient values may vary by sweetness level and preparation. Sweetness level customisation (0%, 25%, 50%, 75%, 100%) is available at KOI Thé; values here assume 100% sugar (standard formulation). Cuisine is classified as Taiwanese per the input record and KOI Thé brand origin; multilingual names follow Singaporean dish rules as this is a Singapore-served item from a Taiwanese brand.
